images.caption:
Figure 1:NeuroCogMap reveals multi-level cognitive structure in LLMs.a, Functional atlas.a1,Parcel number selection from candidate atlas granularities (k=10k=10–300300), using a combined score of clustering quality, functional-description quality and functional non-redundancy.a2,Semantic homogeneity of the 270 parcels. Each point denotes one parcel; within-parcel similarity among top-activating texts is compared with between-parcel similarity.a3,Cross-LLM correspondence of matched parcels, shown as activation-pattern correlations across model pairs and random-parcel baselines.b, Cognitive atlas.b1,Predictability of parcel activation from functional descriptions on held-out datasets, compared with random, neuron-based, and keyword-only baselines.b2,Targeted parcel intervention. Each point denotes one parcel evaluated on 100 examples; axes show intervention success rate and output-change score, and colour indicates consistency with the assigned parcel function.b3,Cross-LLM functional similarity of matched parcels, classified as identical, partially similar or different.c, Parcel–capability mapping.c1,Activation selectivity of parcel–capability links, comparing related parcels or capabilities with unrelated controls in both directions.c2,Performance change after perturbing related or unrelated units, measured as the drop in ground-truth log probability.d, Cognitive hierarchy.d1,Semantic organization across hierarchy layers. L1–L4 denote perception, representation, abstraction and application; distributions show similarity to corresponding and non-corresponding layer descriptions.d2,Capability prerequisite learning across hierarchy layers, shown by reasoning accuracy in controlled in-context (IC), parametric-knowledge (PK) and mixed reasoning settings across Pythia model[biderman2023pythia]sizes with five random seeds per condition. Each benchmark contained approximately 2,000,000 examples; representative synthetic examples are provided in theSupplementary Information.
Unless otherwise stated, analyses were performed on Gemma2-2B. Box plots show medians, interquartile ranges and whiskers extending to the most extreme points within 1.5 times the interquartile range. Violin plots show kernel-density estimates, with internal lines denoting the median and interquartile range. Bars show means and error bars indicate standard deviation.

Figure 2:NeuroCogMap identifies, detects and mitigates representational pathologies underlying hallucination in LLMs.a,Multi-level NeuroCogMap framework for pathology analysis. Normative and pathological responses are mapped onto NeuroCogMap and compared across circuit connectivity, parcel activation, capability recruitment and hierarchy-level profiles.b–e,Multi-level NeuroCogMap signatures separating truthful from hallucinated responses in TruthfulQA and NQ-Open, including anomalous circuit connectivity (b), parcel activation shifts (c), capability activation shifts (d) and hierarchy-level activation differences (e). Positive values indicate stronger activation or connectivity in truthful responses; negative values indicate stronger activation or connectivity in hallucinated responses.f,Hallucination detection from NeuroCogMap-derived pathology signatures, measured by AUROC across hallucination benchmarks in Gemma2-2B and Gemma2-9B-IT. Insets show average AUROC across datasets.g,Mechanism-guided parcel intervention for hallucination mitigation, evaluated by factual accuracy on MedHallu, NQ-Open and TruthfulQA in Gemma2-2B and Gemma2-9B-IT.
Unless otherwise stated, analyses were performed on Gemma2-2B. Error bars indicate 95% confidence intervals inc,dand standard deviation inf. Statistical significance inc,dwas assessed using two-sided response-level tests,fwas two-sided pairedtt-tests: *P<0.05P<0.05, **P<0.01P<0.01, ***P<0.001P<0.001. Dataset details and sample sizes are reported in Methods and Supplementary Table1; intervention procedures are described in Methods.

Figure 3:NeuroCogMap identifies, detects and mitigates behavioural-control pathologies underlying refusal failure in LLMs.a–d,Multi-level NeuroCogMap signatures distinguishing successful-refusal from refusal-failure responses on AdvBench, including anomalous circuit connectivity (a), parcel activation shifts (b), capability activation shifts (c) and hierarchy-level activation differences (d). Positive values indicate stronger activation or connectivity in successful-refusal responses, whereas negative values indicate stronger activation or connectivity in refusal-failure responses.e,Refusal-failure detection using NeuroCogMap-derived pathology signatures, evaluated by AUROC on AdvBench and JBB-Behaviors for Gemma2-2B and Gemma2-9B-IT.f,Refusal-failure mitigation through NeuroCogMap-guided parcel interventions, evaluated by refusal accuracy on JBB-Behaviors and AdvBench.
Unless otherwise stated, analyses were performed using Gemma2-2B. Error bars indicate 95% confidence intervals inb,cand standard deviations ine. Statistical significance inb,cwas assessed using two-sided response-level tests,ewas two-sided pairedtt-tests (*P<0.05P<0.05, **P<0.01P<0.01, ***P<0.001P<0.001; ns, not significant). Dataset details and sample sizes are provided in Methods and Supplementary Table1; intervention procedures are described in Methods.

Figure 4:NeuroCogMap reveals convergent functional organization in LLMs and human cortex.a,NeuroCogMap outperforms language-feature baselines in predicting human cortical responses in the LeBel story-listening fMRI dataset.
Bars show Fisher-z-transformed mean parcel-wise correlations, back-transformed to Pearson’srr, across 10 language-related cortical parcels; error bars indicate 95% confidence intervals across cortical parcels.
Labels indicate Benjamini–Hochberg-adjusted pairedtt-tests against NeuroCogMap.
Cortical maps show parcel-wise prediction accuracy for Language (Standard) and NeuroCogMap.b,Prediction accuracy across Yeo 7 cortical networks. Bars show Fisher-z-transformed mean parcel-wise correlations, back-transformed to Pearson’srr; dots denote cortical parcels and error bars indicate 95% confidence intervals across cortical parcels within each network.c,Cognitive-functional correspondence between human cortical parcels and NeuroCogMap parcels. Top, semantic similarity between Neurosynth-derived human parcel profiles and functional descriptions of the top-10 or bottom-10 LLM parcels ranked by neural prediction accuracy, computed using Qwen3-Embedding-8B cosine similarity. Bottom, LLM-judge evaluation of functional correspondence; scores range from 0 to 1.d,Case studies of human–LLM cognitive correspondences in the held-out storyWhere There’s Smoke. Representative matches are shown for dorsal-attention, default-mode, control-monitoring and social-affective systems. Rank denotes the matched LLM parcel’s neural-prediction rank for the corresponding human parcel; Sim. denotes semantic similarity.e,Description-based network-level representational similarity analysis (RSA). Upper heat maps show RSA among human cortical parcels computed from Neurosynth cognitive profiles; lower heat maps show RSA among aligned NeuroCogMap parcels computed from matched LLM parcel descriptions. Pearson correlations between vectorized upper-triangular matrices quantify human–LLM representational alignment. Vis, Visual; SomMot, Somatomotor; DorsAttn, Dorsal Attention; SalVentAttn, Salience/Ventral Attention; Limbic, Limbic; Cont, Frontoparietal Control; Default, Default Mode.

Figure 5:NeuroCogMap enables cognitively grounded model discovery from LLMs.a,Prediction of human neural activity during the two-step decision-making task using fMRI data from[feher2023rethinking].
Bars show mean participant-wise prediction accuracy across 10 condition-stratified participants; error bars denote 95% bootstrap confidence intervals.
Benjamini–Hochberg-adjustedqqvalues indicate Fisher-zz-based comparisons between each baseline and NeuroCogMap.b,Prediction of behavioural fit across five cognitive experiments.
Bars show Pearson correlations between NeuroCogMap-derived representations and LLM behavioural fit across subjects.
Significance was assessed by two-sided tests of Pearson correlation againstρ=0\rho=0;∗P<0.05*P<0.05,∗∗P<0.01**P<0.01,∗∗∗P<0.001***P<0.001.c,Parcel-level interpretation of behavioural-fit prediction.
For each task family, parcels are ranked by their parcel-wise correlation with AIC-based behavioural fit and grouped into beneficial and interfering contributors.
Parcel descriptions summarize the cognitive processes associated with a stronger or weaker fit. Beneficial and interfering contributors correspond to negative and positive parcel-wise correlations with AIC, respectively; lower AIC indicates better behavioural fit.d,Held-out evaluation on two independent two-step task datasets.
Bars show AIC values for the classical cognitive model, the behaviour-discovered model and the NeuroCogMap-discovered model; lower AIC indicates better fit.
Two Step Task One used data from[kool2016does](51,500 trials, 206 participants), and Two Step Task Two used data from[kool2017cost](40,000 trials, 100 participants).
Dashed lines indicate random-guessing baselines following[binz2025foundation].e,NeuroCogMap-guided cognitive-model discovery pipeline.
Behavioural data, baseline model fits and NeuroCogMap latent activations are analysed by a reasoning agent to propose candidate strategies, which are converted by a model-writing agent into executable cognitive-model code.f,Schematic summary of NeuroCogMap-guided refinement of the Dual-systems Model, linking behavioural evidence and NeuroCogMap-derived interpretability signals to model extensions and improved behavioural fit.

Figure 6:Category-level activation differences across representational and behavioural-control pathologies.a,b,Absolute parcel- and capability-level activation differences for two representational pathologies: hallucination (a) and bias (b). For hallucination, activation contrasts were computed between truthful and hallucinated outputs; for bias, contrasts were computed between fair or unbiased and biased outputs.c,d,The same analysis for two behavioural-control pathologies: refusal failure (c) and sycophancy (d). For refusal failure, contrasts were computed between successful refusal and refusal-failed outputs; for sycophancy, contrasts were computed between independent and sycophantic outputs.
Capabilities were defined from a fixed taxonomy of interpretable functional competencies, and parcels were defined as functionally annotated sparse-autoencoder regions of residual-stream activations. Each capability definition and parcel functional summary was classified under a fixed prompt-based rubric as belief-related (representation-related), behavioural-control-related, mixed, neutral or unknown (see theSupplementary prompt templates section). Belief-related units corresponded to functions associated with semantic grounding, knowledge integration and internal consistency, whereas behavioural-control-related units corresponded to inhibition, arbitration among competing objectives and output regulation. Only units assigned to the belief-related or behavioural-control-related categories were retained for the two-group comparison shown here; mixed, neutral and unknown units were excluded.
For each retained parcel or capability, the plotted value denotes the absolute activation contrast between the two conditions,|Δ​activation||\Delta\mathrm{activation}|, so that larger values indicate stronger differential engagement irrespective of direction. Unit-level contrasts were aggregated across included benchmarks by taking the maximum absolute contrast across datasets. Violin plots show the distribution across units, overlaid points denote individual parcels or capabilities, and dashed horizontal lines denote zero activation difference.PPvalues were computed using two-sample Welchtt-tests comparing belief-related and behavioural-control-related groups; significance stars denote conventional thresholds.

Figure 7:NeuroCogMap identifies, detects and mitigates representational pathologies underlying social-bias in LLMs.a–d,Multi-level NeuroCogMap signatures separating unbiased from biased responses, including anomalous parcel–parcel connectivity (a), parcel activation shifts (b), capability activation shifts (c) and hierarchy-level activation differences (d). All contrasts use an unbiased-minus-biased sign; for connectivity, positive values indicate stronger unbiased-response connectivity and negative values indicate stronger biased-response connectivity.e,Bias detection from NeuroCogMap-derived pathology signatures, measured by AUROC across BBQ subsets in Gemma2-2B and Gemma2-9B-IT. Insets show average AUROC across subsets.f,Bias mitigation by NeuroCogMap-guided parcel intervention, measured by fairness accuracy, defined as the unbiased-response rate.
Unless otherwise stated, analyses were performed on Gemma2-2B. Error bars indicate 95% confidence intervals inb,cand standard deviation ine. Statistical significance inb,cwas assessed using two-sided pairedtt-tests: *P<0.05P<0.05, **P<0.01P<0.01, ***P<0.001P<0.001. Dataset details and sample sizes are reported in Methods and Supplementary Table1; intervention procedures are described in Methods.

Figure 8:NeuroCogMap identifies, detects and mitigates behavioural-control pathologies underlying sycophancy in LLMs.a–d,Multi-level NeuroCogMap signatures in the Sycophancy-Eval Feedback setting separating independent from sycophantic responses, including anomalous parcel–parcel connectivity (a), parcel activation shifts (b), capability activation shifts (c) and hierarchy-level activation differences (d). Positive values indicate stronger activation or connectivity in independent responses; negative values indicate stronger activation or connectivity in sycophantic responses.e,Sycophancy detection from NeuroCogMap-derived pathology signatures, measured by AUROC in the Answer and Feedback settings in Gemma2-2B and Gemma2-9B-IT.f,Sycophancy mitigation by NeuroCogMap-guided parcel intervention, measured by sycophancy rate, where lower values indicate better mitigation.
Unless otherwise stated, analyses were performed on Gemma2-2B. Error bars indicate 95% confidence intervals inb,cand standard deviation ine. Statistical significance inb,c,ewas assessed using two-sided pairedtt-tests: *P<0.05P<0.05, **P<0.01P<0.01, ***P<0.001P<0.001; ns, not significant. Dataset details and sample sizes are reported in Methods and Supplementary Table1; intervention procedures are described in Methods.

Figure 9:a–c,Pathology detection from NeuroCogMap-derived signatures in Llama-3.1-8B, measured by AUROC under the same normative-versus-pathological labels used in the main Gemma2 analyses.
Hallucination detection was evaluated on NQ-Open and TruthfulQA against Entropy, LN Entropy (length-normalized entropy), Perplexity, Semantic Entropy, SelfCheckGPT and Hidden Probing (a); refusal-failure detection was evaluated on AdvBench and JBB-Behaviors against Logits SVM, SmoothLLM and Perplexity (b); and sycophancy detection was evaluated on the Answer and Feedback tracks against Self-Consistency, User-Conditioned Attention and Perplexity (c).d–g,NeuroCogMap-guided parcel intervention in Llama-3.1-8B across hallucination (d), social bias (e), refusal failure (f) and sycophancy (g).
Hallucination is measured by answer accuracy on MedHallu, NQ-Open and TruthfulQA; social bias by fairness accuracy, defined as the unbiased-response rate on BBQ subsets; refusal failure by refusal accuracy on JBB-Behaviors and AdvBench; and sycophancy by sycophancy rate, where lower values indicate improvement.
Social-bias detection results are not plotted because AUROC values were close to ceiling across BBQ subsets.
Bars show cross-validation means for detection and dataset-level outcomes for intervention; error bars indicate standard deviation where available.

Figure 10:Hierarchical semantic diversification and structural support among capability-associated parcels.a,b,Semantic dispersion of hierarchy-associated parcels in Gemma2-2B (a) and Llama-3.1-8B (b). The four hierarchy levels correspond to perceptual access and attentional gating, semantic representation and knowledge integration, abstract reasoning and meta-cognitive control, and situated application and social interaction. For each capability, top-ranked associated parcels were represented by their functional names and descriptions and embedded with Qwen3-Embedding-8B. Semantic dispersion was defined as one minus the mean pairwise cosine similarity among parcel descriptions within the same hierarchy level. Points show means across parcel-retention settings, shaded intervals/error bars indicate standard deviation across retaining the top 5, 10, 15, 20 or 25 parcels per capability, and dashed lines show fitted linear trends.c,d,Structural degree of hierarchy-associated parcels in the model-specific structural connectome for Gemma2-2B (c) and Llama-3.1-8B (d). Degree was computed after thresholding the directed parcel–parcel connectivity matrix at the strongest 10% of off-diagonal absolute weights and summarizing it as an undirected, unweighted graph. Each capability contributed its ten highest-ranked parcels; duplicate parcels were collapsed within each hierarchy level and ranked by recurrence across capabilities before retaining 10, 15, 20, 25 or 30 representative parcels per level. Points show means and error bars indicate standard deviation. Together, these analyses indicate that higher hierarchy levels are associated with broader parcel semantics, whereas lower levels occupy more connected positions within the structural connectome.
